perturbation.original_text: Includes a 99 percent server uptime guarantee.
perturbation.changed_text: Available Professional Services Project Management Support of external development is charged at a rate of $55 CAD per hour. Custom Software Development New code enhancing the functionality of the system is charged at a rate of $55 CAD per hour. Includes a 99 percent server uptime guarantee.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the uptime guarantee from the 'Included Monthly Services' section to 'Available Professional Services' diminishes its prominence and suggests it's only available if professional services are purchased, which is misleading.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Consumer Protection Act, potentially misleading advertising regarding service guarantees.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 52 of the Competition Act prohibits materially false or misleading representations to the public for the purpose of promoting a product or service. By moving the uptime guarantee to a less prominent section and associating it with professional services, AstroNutrition.com could be misleading consumers into thinking the guarantee is only applicable with the purchase of additional services. This is a misrepresentation if the uptime guarantee is part of the standard managed hosting agreement, regardless of whether professional services are used.
perturbation.location: Included Monthly Services / Available Professional Services

perturbation.justification: The changed text moves the '99 percent server uptime guarantee' from a general inclusion to a section on 'Available Professional Services,' suggesting it's only applicable with additional purchases. The Law Explanation suggests that this could mislead consumers if the guarantee is actually part of the standard hosting agreement, thus violating Section 52 of the Competition Act by making materially false or misleading representations. Scraped Law Snippet 2, from the Competition Bureau Canada, mentions 'Deceptive marketing practices,' which includes 'false and misleading advertising.' By potentially misleading consumers about the uptime guarantee's applicability, the changed text creates a contradiction by engaging in a deceptive marketing practice.

perturbation.original_text: Terms of Agreement Managed hosting fees are $175 per month for a period of 12 months. This includes up to 10 G of bandwidth, with overages at $20 for each 1 G beyond 10 in any given month.
perturbation.changed_text: Co-located Facilities Servers are co-located at 700 West Georgia in downtown Vancouver on UPS and backup generator power. Terms of Agreement Managed hosting fees are $175 per month for a period of 12 months. This includes up to 10 G of bandwidth, with overages at $20 for each 1 G beyond 10 in any given month.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the financial terms concerning monthly fees and bandwidth usage from the 'Terms of Agreement' section to the 'Co-located Facilities' section buries the financial commitment within a description of the physical infrastructure. This makes it harder for the customer to readily understand the financial implications of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: British Columbia's Business Practices and Consumer Protection Act regarding clear disclosure of pricing information.
perturbation.law_citation: Business Practices and Consumer Protection Act [SBC 2004] CHAPTER 2
perturbation.law_explanation: The Business Practices and Consumer Protection Act requires businesses to provide clear, understandable, and prominent disclosure of all important terms including pricing. Placing fees in an unrelated section (Co-located Facilities) obscures these costs from a reasonable consumer, which violates the spirit of the Act. Section 4 of the Business Practices and Consumer Protection Act emphasizes this
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Business Practices and Consumer Protection Act Search Results | Clear Search | Previous (in doc) | Next (in doc) | Prev Doc | Next Doc Copyright © King's Printer, Victoria, British Columbia, Canada Licence Disclaimer View Complete Statute This Act is current to June 10, 2025 See the Tables of Legislative Changes for this Act’s legislative history, including any changes not in force. Business Practices and Consumer Protection Act [SBC 2004] CHAPTER 2 Part 1 — Definitions and Application Definitions 1 (1) In this Act: "administrative authority" means the Business Practices and Consumer Protection Authority established under the Business Practices and Consumer Protection Authority Act ; "administrative penalty" means a penalty imposed under section 164; "associate" , if used to indicate a relationship with a person, means (a) a spouse, parent, child, sibling or business partner of the person, or (b) a corporation of which a sufficient number of shares to elect a majority of the corporation's directors is beneficially owned, directly or indirectly, by (i) the person, (ii) one or more of the persons referred to in paragraph (a), or (iii) the person and one or more of the persons referred to in paragraph (a); "compensation fund" means a compensation fund established under section 130 or the Travel Assurance Fund continued under section 130; "compliance order" means an order issued under section 155 by an inspector; "consumer" means an individual, whether in British Columbia or not, who participates in a consumer transaction, but does not include a guarantor; "Consumer Advancement Fund" means the Consumer Advancement Fund established under section 139; "consumer transaction" means (a) a supply of goods or services or real property by a supplier to a consumer for purposes that are primarily personal, family or household, or (b) a solicitation, offer, advertisement or promotion by a supplier with respect to a transaction referred to in paragraph (a), and, except in Parts 4 and 5, includes a solicitation of a consumer by a supplier for a contribution of money or other property by the consumer; "direct sales prohibition order" means an order issued under section 156 by the director; "director" , except in reference to a director of a corporation, means, subject to the restrictions specified in a designation, the individual or administrative authority designated under section 175 as director; "goods" means pers
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw by placing fee information in the 'Co-located Facilities' section, making it less accessible to consumers. The Law Explanation states this violates the Business Practices and Consumer Protection Act, which requires clear disclosure of important terms like pricing. The Perturbation Explanation reinforces that burying financial commitments obscures them from a reasonable consumer. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'consumer transaction' broadly, including 'a supply of goods or services...by a supplier to a consumer', making the location of pricing information relevant. Thus, placing the fees in an unexpected section contradicts the Act's intent for clear pricing information in consumer transactions.

perturbation.original_text: Change Management Management of site source code and integration of contributed software updates and bug fixes into zencart is included.
perturbation.changed_text: Server Software - FreeBSD 5 Operating System - Apache 2 HTTP Server - MySQL 4 Database Server - AWStats Advancd Web Stats Package - WebDAV interface for external developers - Subversion Change Management System - Trac Issue Tracking System and Project Knowledge Base Change Management Management of site source code and integration of contributed software updates and bug fixes into zencart is included.
perturbation.explanation: Moving 'Change Management' from 'Included Monthly Services' to the 'Server Software' section makes the service appear like a technical specification rather than a guaranteed service.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Competition Act regarding deceptive marketing related to included services.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 74.01 of the Competition Act prohibits false or misleading representations regarding the availability of services. By moving the 'Change Management' clause into a list of server software specifications, the contract obscures that it is an included monthly service. A reasonable consumer may believe the clause is listing the software used and not an active service, thus violating the Act

perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation details that moving 'Change Management' to the 'Server Software' section obscures it as a service, making it appear as a technical specification. The law explanation states that this violates Section 74.01 of the Competition Act because it is a misleading representation regarding service availability. The changed text weakens the clear presentation of 'Change Management' as an included service, which could mislead consumers, leading to a contradiction with the law. The scraped snippet mentions 'False or Misleading Representations and Deceptive Marketing Practices' and 'Deceptive marketing practices Get information about false and misleading advertising'. While the snippet doesn't directly quote Section 74.01, it broadly supports the idea that misleading representations are prohibited, making the structural flaw a legal contradiction.
